medical.title: Study finds shared genetic roots of multiple sclerosis across diverse ancestries
medical.summary: A new study published in Neurology, led by Queen Mary University of London, has revealed that people of South Asian, African and European ancestry share many of the same genetic risk factors for multiple sclerosis (MS).

medical.title: Team approach, low-cost interventions lead to major reduction in opioid prescriptions after surgery
medical.summary: A novel team approach founded on a culture change among clinicians and doctor-patient communication successfully reduced post-surgical opioid use, a new study led by University of Rochester Medical Center (URMC) researchers shows.

medical.title: That difficult person in your life could be aging you 1.5% faster, study finds
medical.summary: Spending time with someone who constantly causes problems may do more than just ruin your mood.

medical.title: Mouse study sheds light on how the brain recognizes stable patterns in changing scenes
medical.summary: Humans and many other animals can innately recognize familiar objects in their surroundings, irrespective of the angle they are observed from, changes in lighting or other shifts in the surrounding environment. This ability is known to be supported by a process known as generalization, which allows the brain to respond similarly to different sensory inputs that share some common important features.

medical.title: Study reveals worrying extent of imprecise gene and gene mutation naming
medical.summary: A systematic review of 52 scientific papers submitted to a world-leading clinical genetics journal from multiple scientists over a two-year period reveals that not a single one named critical gene mutations (correctly termed as variants) with precision. The findings partly explain why about 70% of rare diseases go undiagnosed, even in the U.K., which arguably has the world's most advanced genomic medicine service. Led by Dr. Peter Freeman, a geneticist from The University of Manchester, the findings are published in Clinical Chemistry.

medical.title: Immune cells play key role in regulating eye pressure linked to glaucoma, research reveals
medical.summary: When the eye's drainage system clogs, pressure builds up and causes damage. The pressure can lead to glaucoma and vision loss. New research, published in the journal Immunity, reveals that a specialized set of immune cells act as the cleanup crew, pointing to a promising new target for therapies to prevent a major cause of blindness.

medical.title: Biomimetic platform developed to enhance CAR T cell therapy against leukemia
medical.summary: Chimeric antigen receptor T (CAR T) cell therapy represents a milestone in leukemia treatment. CAR T works by genetically engineering a chimeric antigen receptor on the surface of the patient's T cells to target specific antigens on leukemia cells, with the goal of identifying and eliminating them. However, clinical data show that more than 50% of patients eventually relapse after CAR T treatment. One major reason is that leukemia cells can reduce or lose expression of the targeted antigen under therapeutic pressure. When this occurs, CAR T cells can no longer effectively recognize and eliminate leukemia cells.

medical.title: Out-of-state abortion care: Patient interviews reveal delays and stigma
medical.summary: State-level abortion restrictions have shifted the landscape of care and the experiences of people traveling for abortion care after the June 2022 Dobbs v Jackson Women's Health Organization Supreme Court decision. A new, qualitative study published JAMA Network Open takes a deeper look at the experiences of people traveling from U.S. states with abortion restrictions or bans to Illinois, a state where abortion remains legal.

medical.title: Policy change tied to higher kidney transplant rates for Black patients
medical.summary: A new national study evaluating a landmark U.S. transplant policy change finds that efforts to correct the harms of race-based kidney function equations are associated with increased kidney transplantation rates among Black patients. The study, published in JAMA Internal Medicine, underscores how reparative strategies that address the harms of race-based algorithms in medicine can help save lives.

science.title: How boron helps to produce key proteins for new cancer therapies
science.summary: Chemists from ETH Zurich have found a way to produce poorly soluble proteins by caging a uniquely reactive boron compound. This method opens up new possibilities for the synthesis of tailored protein therapeutics, including cancer treatments.

science.title: European Space Agency probing fireball that hit German home
science.summary: The European Space Agency said it is investigating a fireball that streaked across the skies of Europe on the weekend before reportedly punching a football-sized hole in the roof of a German home.

science.title: COPV Damage Tolerance Life Demonstration Guidelines
science.summary: This article is from the 2025 Technical Update. The NESC has invested significant time and resources to better understand composite overwrapped pressure vessels (COPV) performance and more importantly, how these complex, high-pressure storage systems can fail. These vessels, which store high pressure propulsion and life-support system fluids on launch vehicles and spacecraft, are ubiquitous at NASA, and failures have […]

science.title: A Technical Resource for the Agency
science.summary: This article is from the 2025 Technical Update. The NESC’s Thermal Control & Protection Technical Discipline Team (TDT) is a resource providing subject matter expertise in active and passive thermal control as well as ascent and entry thermal protection across the spectrum of agency needs. TDT members led or supported a variety of key activities […]

science.title: Celebrating NASA’s Mars Reconnaissance Orbiter’s 20th Anniversary: Crater Near Sirenum Fossae
science.summary: NASA’s Mars Reconnaissance Orbiter (MRO) captures a detailed view of a relatively fresh crater in this image released on June 3, 2015. The crater has a sharp rim and well-preserved ejecta. The steep inner slopes are carved by gullies and include possible recurring slope lineae on the equator-facing slopes. This crater is monitored for changes […]

science.title: Why March Madness is a perfect storm for betting
science.summary: Sports betting continues to explode across the country. Online gambling platforms have become mainstream, are heavily marketed by celebrities and star athletes—and increasingly popular among young adults.

science.title: Ancient parrot DNA reveals sophisticated, long-distance animal trade network pre-dating the Inca Empire
science.summary: New analysis of ancient parrot DNA has revealed that vibrant Amazonian parrots were transported alive across the Andes to coastal Peru centuries before the Inca Empire, highlighting a sophisticated pre-Inca, long-distance trade network spanning rainforest, highlands and deserts. The international team of researchers, including scientists from The Australian National University (ANU), analyzed parrot feathers that were discovered at Pachacamac, Peru—one of the preeminent religious centers of the Andean civilization—far outside the birds' native rainforest range. The research is published in Nature Communications.

science.title: Antibiotic resistance can vary depending on where the bacteria live
science.summary: New research from the Technical University of Denmark indicates that the outcome of a resistance measurement may depend on the conditions under which the bacterium is tested. Standard laboratory tests are carried out under fixed, uniform conditions, but if, for example, the test environment is altered, the very same bacterium may in some cases be either more or less susceptible to an antibiotic than the laboratory result indicates. The research is published in the journal Applied and Environmental Microbiology.

science.title: NASA Discovers Crash of Extreme Stars in Unexpected Site
science.summary: A fleet of NASA missions has likely uncovered a collision between two ultradense stars in a tiny galaxy buried in a huge stream of gas. Astronomers have never seen this type of explosive event in an environment like this before — and it may help solve two outstanding cosmic mysteries. A paper describing these results […]

environment.title: The hidden technology that could unlock commercial fusion power
environment.summary: Fusion energy may be one of the most promising clean power sources of the future—but only if scientists can precisely measure the extreme, fast-moving plasmas that make it possible. A new U.S. Department of Energy–sponsored report urges major investment in advanced diagnostic tools—the high-tech “sensors” that track plasma temperature, density, and behavior inside fusion systems. Bringing together 70 experts from universities, national labs, and private industry, the workshop identified seven priority areas ranging from burning plasma to full-scale pilot plants.

environment.title: Ancient mystery on K’gari: World’s largest sand island lakes dried up during rainy era
environment.summary: K’gari’s iconic lakes have existed for tens of thousands of years—but they haven’t always been full. New research shows that about 7,500 years ago, during a time of high rainfall, several of the island’s deepest lakes mysteriously vanished. Scientists believe changing wind patterns may have redirected rain away from the island. As the climate shifts again, the lakes’ long-term survival is no longer guaranteed.

environment.source: NASA Earth Observatory - Image of the Day
environment.title: Scoria Cones on Earth and Mars
environment.summary: The hill-shaped features are a sign of explosive volcanic activity—a rarity on the Red Planet. The post Scoria Cones on Earth and Mars appeared first on NASA Science .

environment.title: A major climate hope in Antarctica just melted away
environment.summary: A popular climate theory suggested that melting Antarctic glaciers would release iron into the ocean, sparking algae blooms that pull carbon dioxide from the air. New field data from West Antarctica reveal that meltwater provides far less iron than scientists once believed. Instead, most of the iron comes from deep ocean water and sediments, not from the melting ice itself. The discovery raises new questions about how Antarctica influences climate change.

environment.source: NASA Earth Observatory - Image of the Day
environment.title: Chesapeake Bay Locked in Ice
environment.summary: Nearly 50 years ago, the first Landsat satellite captured the rare sight of Mid-Atlantic waterways frozen over. The post Chesapeake Bay Locked in Ice appeared first on NASA Science .

environment.title: Antarctica just saw the fastest glacier collapse ever recorded
environment.summary: Antarctica’s Hektoria Glacier stunned scientists by retreating eight kilometers in just two months, with nearly half of it collapsing in record time. The rapid breakup was driven by a flat, underwater bedrock surface that allowed the glacier to suddenly float and fracture from below. Satellite and seismic data captured the dramatic chain reaction in near real time. The findings raise concerns that much larger glaciers could one day collapse just as quickly.

environment.title: Congo basin blackwater lakes are releasing ancient carbon into the atmosphere
environment.summary: Deep in the Congo Basin, vast peatlands quietly store enormous amounts of Earth’s carbon — but new research suggests this ancient vault may be leaking. Scientists studying Africa’s largest blackwater lakes discovered that significant amounts of carbon dioxide bubbling into the atmosphere come not just from recent plant life, but from peat that has been locked away for thousands of years.

environment.source: NASA Earth Observatory - Image of the Day
environment.title: Showy Swirls Around Jeju Island
environment.summary: Winds blowing past the volcanic landmass near the Korean Peninsula created a trail of spiraling clouds, while murky water churned nearby. The post Showy Swirls Around Jeju Island appeared first on NASA Science .

environment.source: NASA Earth Observatory - Image of the Day
environment.title: Curiosity Studies Nodules on Boxwork Formations
environment.summary: Description NASA’s Curiosity Mars rover discovered these bumpy, pea-sized nodules while exploring a region filled with boxwork formations — low ridges standing roughly 3 to 6 feet (1 to 2 meters) tall with sandy hollows in-between. This mosaic is made up of 50 individual images taken by Curiosity’s Mars Hand Lens Imager (MAHLI), a camera on […] The post Curiosity Studies Nodules on Boxwork Formations appeared first on NASA Science .

environment.title: Curiosity Surveys the Boxwork Region
environment.summary: Description NASA’s Curiosity Mars rover captured this panorama of boxwork formations — the low ridges seen here with hollows in between them — using its Mastcam on Sept. 26, 2025, the 4,671st Martian day, or sol, of the mission. These boxwork formations were created billions of years ago when water leaked through rock cracks. Minerals […] The post Curiosity Surveys the Boxwork Region appeared first on NASA Science .

environment.title: Schrödinger’s color theory finally completed after 100 years
environment.summary: A century after Erwin Schrödinger sketched out a bold vision for how we perceive color, scientists have finally filled in the missing pieces. A Los Alamos team used advanced geometry to show that hue, saturation, and lightness aren’t shaped by culture or experience — they’re built directly into the mathematical structure of how we see color. By defining a crucial missing element known as the “neutral axis,” the researchers repaired a long-standing flaw in Schrödinger’s model and even corrected tricky visual quirks like the way brightness can subtly shift perceived hue.

environment.title: A giant blade-crested spinosaurus, the “hell heron,” discovered in the Sahara
environment.summary: Deep in the heart of the Sahara, scientists have uncovered Spinosaurus mirabilis — a spectacular new predator crowned with a massive, scimitar-shaped crest that may once have blazed with color under the desert sun. Discovered in remote inland river deposits in Niger, the fossil rewrites what we thought we knew about spinosaur dinosaurs, suggesting they weren’t fully aquatic hunters but powerful waders stalking fish in forested waterways hundreds of miles from the sea.

environment.title: Arctic Blast Brightened the West Florida Shelf
environment.summary: A cold snap in the southern U.S. stirred up a dazzling display of sediment in coastal waters. The post Arctic Blast Brightened the West Florida Shelf appeared first on NASA Science .

environment.title: Triceratops had a giant nose that may have cooled its massive head
environment.summary: Triceratops’ massive head may have been doing more than just showing off those famous horns. Using CT scans and 3D reconstructions of fossil skulls, researchers uncovered a surprisingly complex nasal system hidden inside its enormous snout. Instead of being just a supersized nose for smelling, it likely housed intricate networks of nerves and blood vessels—and even special structures that helped regulate heat and moisture.

environment.title: Frozen for 5,000 years, this ice cave bacterium resists modern antibiotics
environment.summary: Deep inside a Romanian ice cave, locked away in a 5,000-year-old layer of ice, scientists have uncovered a bacterium with a startling secret: it’s resistant to many modern antibiotics. Despite predating the antibiotic era, this cold-loving microbe carries more than 100 resistance-related genes and can survive drugs used today to treat serious infections like tuberculosis and UTIs.

environment.title: Scientists uncover oxygen-loving ancestor of all complex life
environment.summary: For decades, scientists have believed that complex life began when two very different microbes joined forces, eventually giving rise to plants, animals, and fungi. But one major puzzle remained: how could these organisms have met if one depended on oxygen and the other supposedly lived without it? New research suggests the answer lies in ancient microbes called Asgard archaea.

environment.title: Winds Whip Up Fires and Dust on the Southern Plains
environment.summary: Dry, gusty conditions spurred fast-growing fires in Oklahoma and Kansas, along with dangerous dust storms across the region. The post Winds Whip Up Fires and Dust on the Southern Plains appeared first on NASA Science .

environment.title: Giant virus discovery could rewrite the origin of complex life
environment.summary: A giant virus discovered in Japan is adding fuel to the provocative idea that viruses helped create complex life. Named ushikuvirus, it infects amoebae and shows unique traits that connect different families of giant DNA viruses. Its unusual way of hijacking and disrupting the host cell’s nucleus offers fresh insight into how viruses may have influenced the evolution of the cell nucleus itself. The finding deepens the mystery of viruses—and their possible role in life’s biggest leap.

environment.title: Ancient DNA solves 5,500 year old burial mystery in Sweden
environment.summary: Ancient DNA from a Stone Age burial site in Sweden shows that families 5,500 years ago were more complex than expected. Many individuals buried together were not immediate family, but second- or third-degree relatives. One grave held a young woman alongside two children who were siblings—yet she wasn’t their mother. The discoveries hint at tight-knit communities where extended kin mattered deeply.

environment.title: New map reveals where lethal scorpions are most likely to strike
environment.summary: Scientists have developed a powerful new way to forecast where some of the world’s most dangerous scorpions are likely to be found. By combining fieldwork in Africa with advanced computer modeling, the team discovered that soil type is the strongest factor shaping where many lethal species live, while temperature patterns also play a key role.

environment.title: A spinning gyroscope could finally unlock ocean wave energy
environment.summary: Ocean waves are a vast and steady source of renewable energy, but capturing their power efficiently has long frustrated engineers. A researcher at The University of Osaka has now explored a bold new approach: a gyroscopic wave energy converter that uses a spinning flywheel inside a floating structure to turn wave motion into electricity. By harnessing gyroscopic precession—the subtle wobble of a spinning object under force—the system can be tuned to absorb energy across a wide range of wave conditions.

environment.title: Ancient microbes may have used oxygen 500 million years before it filled Earth’s atmosphere
environment.summary: Life on Earth may have learned to breathe oxygen long before oxygen filled the skies. MIT researchers traced a key oxygen-processing enzyme back hundreds of millions of years before the Great Oxidation Event. Early microbes living near oxygen-producing cyanobacteria may have quickly used up the gas as it formed, slowing its rise in the atmosphere. The results suggest life was adapting to oxygen far earlier — and far more creatively — than once thought.

mix_items.summary: Sports betting continues to explode across the country. Online gambling platforms have become mainstream, are heavily marketed by celebrities and star athletes—and increasingly popular among young adults.

mix_items.title: Ancient parrot DNA reveals sophisticated, long-distance animal trade network pre-dating the Inca Empire
mix_items.summary: New analysis of ancient parrot DNA has revealed that vibrant Amazonian parrots were transported alive across the Andes to coastal Peru centuries before the Inca Empire, highlighting a sophisticated pre-Inca, long-distance trade network spanning rainforest, highlands and deserts. The international team of researchers, including scientists from The Australian National University (ANU), analyzed parrot feathers that were discovered at Pachacamac, Peru—one of the preeminent religious centers of the Andean civilization—far outside the birds' native rainforest range. The research is published in Nature Communications.

mix_items.title: Antibiotic resistance can vary depending on where the bacteria live
mix_items.summary: New research from the Technical University of Denmark indicates that the outcome of a resistance measurement may depend on the conditions under which the bacterium is tested. Standard laboratory tests are carried out under fixed, uniform conditions, but if, for example, the test environment is altered, the very same bacterium may in some cases be either more or less susceptible to an antibiotic than the laboratory result indicates. The research is published in the journal Applied and Environmental Microbiology.

mix_items.title: NASA Discovers Crash of Extreme Stars in Unexpected Site
mix_items.summary: A fleet of NASA missions has likely uncovered a collision between two ultradense stars in a tiny galaxy buried in a huge stream of gas. Astronomers have never seen this type of explosive event in an environment like this before — and it may help solve two outstanding cosmic mysteries. A paper describing these results […]

mix_items.title: Study finds shared genetic roots of multiple sclerosis across diverse ancestries
mix_items.summary: A new study published in Neurology, led by Queen Mary University of London, has revealed that people of South Asian, African and European ancestry share many of the same genetic risk factors for multiple sclerosis (MS).

mix_items.title: Team approach, low-cost interventions lead to major reduction in opioid prescriptions after surgery
mix_items.summary: A novel team approach founded on a culture change among clinicians and doctor-patient communication successfully reduced post-surgical opioid use, a new study led by University of Rochester Medical Center (URMC) researchers shows.

mix_items.title: That difficult person in your life could be aging you 1.5% faster, study finds
mix_items.summary: Spending time with someone who constantly causes problems may do more than just ruin your mood.

mix_items.title: Mouse study sheds light on how the brain recognizes stable patterns in changing scenes
mix_items.summary: Humans and many other animals can innately recognize familiar objects in their surroundings, irrespective of the angle they are observed from, changes in lighting or other shifts in the surrounding environment. This ability is known to be supported by a process known as generalization, which allows the brain to respond similarly to different sensory inputs that share some common important features.

mix_items.title: Study reveals worrying extent of imprecise gene and gene mutation naming
mix_items.summary: A systematic review of 52 scientific papers submitted to a world-leading clinical genetics journal from multiple scientists over a two-year period reveals that not a single one named critical gene mutations (correctly termed as variants) with precision. The findings partly explain why about 70% of rare diseases go undiagnosed, even in the U.K., which arguably has the world's most advanced genomic medicine service. Led by Dr. Peter Freeman, a geneticist from The University of Manchester, the findings are published in Clinical Chemistry.

mix_items.title: Immune cells play key role in regulating eye pressure linked to glaucoma, research reveals
mix_items.summary: When the eye's drainage system clogs, pressure builds up and causes damage. The pressure can lead to glaucoma and vision loss. New research, published in the journal Immunity, reveals that a specialized set of immune cells act as the cleanup crew, pointing to a promising new target for therapies to prevent a major cause of blindness.

mix_items.title: Biomimetic platform developed to enhance CAR T cell therapy against leukemia
mix_items.summary: Chimeric antigen receptor T (CAR T) cell therapy represents a milestone in leukemia treatment. CAR T works by genetically engineering a chimeric antigen receptor on the surface of the patient's T cells to target specific antigens on leukemia cells, with the goal of identifying and eliminating them. However, clinical data show that more than 50% of patients eventually relapse after CAR T treatment. One major reason is that leukemia cells can reduce or lose expression of the targeted antigen under therapeutic pressure. When this occurs, CAR T cells can no longer effectively recognize and eliminate leukemia cells.

mix_items.title: Out-of-state abortion care: Patient interviews reveal delays and stigma
mix_items.summary: State-level abortion restrictions have shifted the landscape of care and the experiences of people traveling for abortion care after the June 2022 Dobbs v Jackson Women's Health Organization Supreme Court decision. A new, qualitative study published JAMA Network Open takes a deeper look at the experiences of people traveling from U.S. states with abortion restrictions or bans to Illinois, a state where abortion remains legal.

mix_items.title: Policy change tied to higher kidney transplant rates for Black patients
mix_items.summary: A new national study evaluating a landmark U.S. transplant policy change finds that efforts to correct the harms of race-based kidney function equations are associated with increased kidney transplantation rates among Black patients. The study, published in JAMA Internal Medicine, underscores how reparative strategies that address the harms of race-based algorithms in medicine can help save lives.

mix_items.title: The hidden technology that could unlock commercial fusion power
mix_items.summary: Fusion energy may be one of the most promising clean power sources of the future—but only if scientists can precisely measure the extreme, fast-moving plasmas that make it possible. A new U.S. Department of Energy–sponsored report urges major investment in advanced diagnostic tools—the high-tech “sensors” that track plasma temperature, density, and behavior inside fusion systems. Bringing together 70 experts from universities, national labs, and private industry, the workshop identified seven priority areas ranging from burning plasma to full-scale pilot plants.

mix_items.title: Ancient mystery on K’gari: World’s largest sand island lakes dried up during rainy era
mix_items.summary: K’gari’s iconic lakes have existed for tens of thousands of years—but they haven’t always been full. New research shows that about 7,500 years ago, during a time of high rainfall, several of the island’s deepest lakes mysteriously vanished. Scientists believe changing wind patterns may have redirected rain away from the island. As the climate shifts again, the lakes’ long-term survival is no longer guaranteed.

mix_items.source: NASA Earth Observatory - Image of the Day
mix_items.title: Scoria Cones on Earth and Mars
mix_items.summary: The hill-shaped features are a sign of explosive volcanic activity—a rarity on the Red Planet. The post Scoria Cones on Earth and Mars appeared first on NASA Science .

mix_items.title: A major climate hope in Antarctica just melted away
mix_items.summary: A popular climate theory suggested that melting Antarctic glaciers would release iron into the ocean, sparking algae blooms that pull carbon dioxide from the air. New field data from West Antarctica reveal that meltwater provides far less iron than scientists once believed. Instead, most of the iron comes from deep ocean water and sediments, not from the melting ice itself. The discovery raises new questions about how Antarctica influences climate change.

mix_items.source: NASA Earth Observatory - Image of the Day
mix_items.title: Chesapeake Bay Locked in Ice
mix_items.summary: Nearly 50 years ago, the first Landsat satellite captured the rare sight of Mid-Atlantic waterways frozen over. The post Chesapeake Bay Locked in Ice appeared first on NASA Science .

mix_items.title: Antarctica just saw the fastest glacier collapse ever recorded
mix_items.summary: Antarctica’s Hektoria Glacier stunned scientists by retreating eight kilometers in just two months, with nearly half of it collapsing in record time. The rapid breakup was driven by a flat, underwater bedrock surface that allowed the glacier to suddenly float and fracture from below. Satellite and seismic data captured the dramatic chain reaction in near real time. The findings raise concerns that much larger glaciers could one day collapse just as quickly.

mix_items.title: Congo basin blackwater lakes are releasing ancient carbon into the atmosphere
mix_items.summary: Deep in the Congo Basin, vast peatlands quietly store enormous amounts of Earth’s carbon — but new research suggests this ancient vault may be leaking. Scientists studying Africa’s largest blackwater lakes discovered that significant amounts of carbon dioxide bubbling into the atmosphere come not just from recent plant life, but from peat that has been locked away for thousands of years.

mix_items.source: NASA Earth Observatory - Image of the Day
mix_items.title: Showy Swirls Around Jeju Island
mix_items.summary: Winds blowing past the volcanic landmass near the Korean Peninsula created a trail of spiraling clouds, while murky water churned nearby. The post Showy Swirls Around Jeju Island appeared first on NASA Science .

mix_items.source: NASA Earth Observatory - Image of the Day
mix_items.title: Curiosity Studies Nodules on Boxwork Formations
mix_items.summary: Description NASA’s Curiosity Mars rover discovered these bumpy, pea-sized nodules while exploring a region filled with boxwork formations — low ridges standing roughly 3 to 6 feet (1 to 2 meters) tall with sandy hollows in-between. This mosaic is made up of 50 individual images taken by Curiosity’s Mars Hand Lens Imager (MAHLI), a camera on […] The post Curiosity Studies Nodules on Boxwork Formations appeared first on NASA Science .

mix_items.title: Curiosity Surveys the Boxwork Region
mix_items.summary: Description NASA’s Curiosity Mars rover captured this panorama of boxwork formations — the low ridges seen here with hollows in between them — using its Mastcam on Sept. 26, 2025, the 4,671st Martian day, or sol, of the mission. These boxwork formations were created billions of years ago when water leaked through rock cracks. Minerals […] The post Curiosity Surveys the Boxwork Region appeared first on NASA Science .

mix_items.title: Schrödinger’s color theory finally completed after 100 years
mix_items.summary: A century after Erwin Schrödinger sketched out a bold vision for how we perceive color, scientists have finally filled in the missing pieces. A Los Alamos team used advanced geometry to show that hue, saturation, and lightness aren’t shaped by culture or experience — they’re built directly into the mathematical structure of how we see color. By defining a crucial missing element known as the “neutral axis,” the researchers repaired a long-standing flaw in Schrödinger’s model and even corrected tricky visual quirks like the way brightness can subtly shift perceived hue.

mix_items.title: A giant blade-crested spinosaurus, the “hell heron,” discovered in the Sahara
mix_items.summary: Deep in the heart of the Sahara, scientists have uncovered Spinosaurus mirabilis — a spectacular new predator crowned with a massive, scimitar-shaped crest that may once have blazed with color under the desert sun. Discovered in remote inland river deposits in Niger, the fossil rewrites what we thought we knew about spinosaur dinosaurs, suggesting they weren’t fully aquatic hunters but powerful waders stalking fish in forested waterways hundreds of miles from the sea.

mix_items.title: Arctic Blast Brightened the West Florida Shelf
mix_items.summary: A cold snap in the southern U.S. stirred up a dazzling display of sediment in coastal waters. The post Arctic Blast Brightened the West Florida Shelf appeared first on NASA Science .

mix_items.title: Triceratops had a giant nose that may have cooled its massive head
mix_items.summary: Triceratops’ massive head may have been doing more than just showing off those famous horns. Using CT scans and 3D reconstructions of fossil skulls, researchers uncovered a surprisingly complex nasal system hidden inside its enormous snout. Instead of being just a supersized nose for smelling, it likely housed intricate networks of nerves and blood vessels—and even special structures that helped regulate heat and moisture.

mix_items.title: Frozen for 5,000 years, this ice cave bacterium resists modern antibiotics
mix_items.summary: Deep inside a Romanian ice cave, locked away in a 5,000-year-old layer of ice, scientists have uncovered a bacterium with a startling secret: it’s resistant to many modern antibiotics. Despite predating the antibiotic era, this cold-loving microbe carries more than 100 resistance-related genes and can survive drugs used today to treat serious infections like tuberculosis and UTIs.

mix_items.title: Scientists uncover oxygen-loving ancestor of all complex life
mix_items.summary: For decades, scientists have believed that complex life began when two very different microbes joined forces, eventually giving rise to plants, animals, and fungi. But one major puzzle remained: how could these organisms have met if one depended on oxygen and the other supposedly lived without it? New research suggests the answer lies in ancient microbes called Asgard archaea.

mix_items.title: Winds Whip Up Fires and Dust on the Southern Plains
mix_items.summary: Dry, gusty conditions spurred fast-growing fires in Oklahoma and Kansas, along with dangerous dust storms across the region. The post Winds Whip Up Fires and Dust on the Southern Plains appeared first on NASA Science .

mix_items.title: Giant virus discovery could rewrite the origin of complex life
mix_items.summary: A giant virus discovered in Japan is adding fuel to the provocative idea that viruses helped create complex life. Named ushikuvirus, it infects amoebae and shows unique traits that connect different families of giant DNA viruses. Its unusual way of hijacking and disrupting the host cell’s nucleus offers fresh insight into how viruses may have influenced the evolution of the cell nucleus itself. The finding deepens the mystery of viruses—and their possible role in life’s biggest leap.

mix_items.title: Ancient DNA solves 5,500 year old burial mystery in Sweden
mix_items.summary: Ancient DNA from a Stone Age burial site in Sweden shows that families 5,500 years ago were more complex than expected. Many individuals buried together were not immediate family, but second- or third-degree relatives. One grave held a young woman alongside two children who were siblings—yet she wasn’t their mother. The discoveries hint at tight-knit communities where extended kin mattered deeply.

mix_items.title: New map reveals where lethal scorpions are most likely to strike
mix_items.summary: Scientists have developed a powerful new way to forecast where some of the world’s most dangerous scorpions are likely to be found. By combining fieldwork in Africa with advanced computer modeling, the team discovered that soil type is the strongest factor shaping where many lethal species live, while temperature patterns also play a key role.

mix_items.title: A spinning gyroscope could finally unlock ocean wave energy
mix_items.summary: Ocean waves are a vast and steady source of renewable energy, but capturing their power efficiently has long frustrated engineers. A researcher at The University of Osaka has now explored a bold new approach: a gyroscopic wave energy converter that uses a spinning flywheel inside a floating structure to turn wave motion into electricity. By harnessing gyroscopic precession—the subtle wobble of a spinning object under force—the system can be tuned to absorb energy across a wide range of wave conditions.

mix_items.title: Ancient microbes may have used oxygen 500 million years before it filled Earth’s atmosphere
mix_items.summary: Life on Earth may have learned to breathe oxygen long before oxygen filled the skies. MIT researchers traced a key oxygen-processing enzyme back hundreds of millions of years before the Great Oxidation Event. Early microbes living near oxygen-producing cyanobacteria may have quickly used up the gas as it formed, slowing its rise in the atmosphere. The results suggest life was adapting to oxygen far earlier — and far more creatively — than once thought.

joke_ro.text: - M-am culcat devreme. In sensul ca am stat devreme pe telefon.

satire_ro.title: Alertă de apocalipsă zombie în România după ce Ponta şi Oprea au înviat. Urmează Iliescu?
